skill.name: polars
skill.description: High-performance Polars guidance for DataFrame analysis, lazy execution, data I/O, transformations, and pandas migration.
skill.summary: Use Polars patterns for fast DataFrame analytics and data pipeline code.
skill.author: davila7

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are documentation false positives from Markdown code spans, Polars expression names, and ordinary DataFrame terminology. The only confirmed concern is low-risk guidance around file, database, cloud I/O, and placeholder credential environment variables; no malicious intent, prompt injection, command execution, or exfiltration was found.

low_findings.title: Placeholder Cloud Credentials in Documentation
low_findings.description: The I/O guide shows environment-variable examples for AWS, Azure, and Google Cloud credentials. The values are placeholders and no exfiltration code is present, but generated examples should avoid hardcoding secrets and should recommend secure credential handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly assign placeholder credential environment variables. This is legitimate documentation, but it can encourage unsafe secret handling if copied into production code.

low_findings.title: Local and Remote Data Access Examples
low_findings.description: The skill includes examples for reading and writing local files, database URIs, and cloud object paths. These capabilities are expected for a data I/O guide, but users should review generated paths and queries before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation intentionally covers file, database, and cloud I/O. I found no unauthorized access logic, but these examples can affect user data when executed.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Markdown Code Formatting Detected as Shell Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The 647 external command hits are Markdown inline code or fenced Python examples. I found no Ruby backtick execution, shell command construction, or runtime command invocation in the skill content.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Line-numbered review shows documentation snippets and Polars expressions, not executable marketplace code. The scanner appears to treat Markdown backticks as Ruby shell backticks.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Polars list.eval Is Not Dynamic Code Execution
dangerous_patterns.description: The scripts finding points to a Polars expression method, pl.col("items").list.eval(pl.element() > 10). This evaluates a Polars list expression, not arbitrary source text or user-controlled code.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact line uses the Polars list namespace with a typed expression argument. There is no Python eval, JavaScript eval, string compilation, or user input execution.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Keywords Are DataFrame Terms
dangerous_patterns.description: Weak cryptography findings map to terms such as expression evaluation, sorting, and documentation references. I found no MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or custom cryptographic implementation in the cited contexts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are Polars documentation, not cryptographic code. The high-severity scanner labels are semantic mismatches with words like evaluation and descending.

dangerous_patterns.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Patterns Are Performance Terms
dangerous_patterns.description: System reconnaissance hits occur in documentation about Python functions, parallelization, and performance pitfalls. I found no host inspection, process listing, credential discovery, or environment probing behavior.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited snippets discuss DataFrame optimization and reshaping. They do not collect system information or enumerate the execution environment.

content.value_statement: Large data workflows often become slow or memory heavy with row-based patterns. This skill helps Claude, Codex, and Claude Code produce Polars code that uses expressions, lazy execution, and efficient I/O.
actual_capabilities: Explains Polars expressions, eager DataFrames, lazy LazyFrames, and query optimization patterns.
Provides examples for selecting, filtering, grouping, joining, sorting, and window operations.
Guides CSV, Parquet, JSON, Excel, database, cloud storage, Arrow, pandas, and NumPy I/O workflows.
Maps common pandas operations to equivalent Polars patterns for migration projects.
Highlights performance practices such as predicate pushdown, streaming, type control, and vectorized expressions.
limitations: It is documentation only and does not install or run Polars by itself.
Examples use placeholder paths, database URIs, and cloud credentials that users must replace securely.
It does not validate data schemas or benchmark pipelines automatically.
Version-specific Polars behavior may require checking the installed package documentation.

use_cases.title: Migrate pandas analysis to Polars
use_cases.description: Convert common pandas selection, filtering, aggregation, and reshaping patterns into Polars expressions.

use_cases.title: Design efficient data pipelines
use_cases.description: Plan lazy scans, early filters, Parquet output, and streaming operations for large datasets.

use_cases.title: Optimize transformation code
use_cases.description: Replace row iteration and Python callbacks with native Polars expressions and grouped computations.

prompt_templates.title: Create a basic Polars workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Polars workflow that reads a CSV file, selects useful columns, filters invalid rows, and returns a summary table. Explain each step briefly.

prompt_templates.title: Convert pandas code to Polars
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this pandas workflow to Polars. Preserve the output shape, note syntax differences, and replace row-wise operations with expressions where possible.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a lazy data pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a lazy Polars pipeline for a large Parquet dataset. Use predicate pushdown, projection pushdown, grouped aggregation, and streaming where appropriate.

prompt_templates.title: Review Polars performance
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this Polars pipeline for performance and correctness. Identify eager reads, avoidable Python functions, inefficient joins, schema risks, and better expression patterns.

output_examples.input: A request to summarize monthly sales by region from a large CSV file.
output_examples.output: A lazy Polars pipeline plan that scans the file, selects required columns, filters bad rows early, groups by month and region, and writes compressed Parquet output.

output_examples.input: A pandas workflow that uses apply, groupby, merge, and pivot_table.
output_examples.output: A migration explanation that maps each operation to Polars expressions, joins, group_by aggregations, and pivot operations with notes on parallel execution.

output_examples.input: A slow Polars script that loads every column before filtering.
output_examples.output: A performance review recommending lazy scans, early column projection, predicate pushdown, explicit data types, and avoiding Python callbacks in hot paths.

best_practices: Use lazy scans for large files and filter or select columns as early as possible.
Prefer native Polars expressions over Python functions for parallel execution and optimizer support.
Store secrets outside examples and use secure credential management for cloud and database access.
anti_patterns: Do not iterate through rows for transformations that can be expressed with Polars expressions.
Do not hardcode database passwords or cloud keys in notebooks, scripts, or generated examples.
Do not collect lazy results before filters, projections, joins, and aggregations are defined.

faq.question: What does this skill help with?
faq.answer: It helps generate and review Polars DataFrame code for analysis, transformations, I/O, performance, and pandas migration.

faq.question: Does it run Polars code automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and examples. The user or agent must run code in an appropriate Python environment.

faq.question: Can it help migrate pandas code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes operation mappings and migration patterns for common pandas workflows.

faq.question: Does it support large datasets?
faq.answer: Yes. The guidance covers lazy execution, streaming, Parquet, pushdown optimization, and memory reduction patterns.

faq.question: Are cloud storage examples included?
faq.answer: Yes. The I/O guide includes S3, Azure Blob Storage, Google Cloud Storage, and BigQuery examples with placeholder credentials.

faq.question: What should users check before production use?
faq.answer: Users should validate schemas, manage secrets securely, test with representative data, and confirm behavior for their installed Polars version.
